title: Return-home smell and sound inspection
summary: A safe-edge inspection before entering a home after flood, fire, earthquake, storm, gas concern, or long absence. It uses smell, sound, water, heat, and animals as warning signals.
content:
A safe-edge inspection before entering a home after flood, fire, earthquake, storm, gas concern, or long absence. It uses smell, sound, water, heat, and animals as warning signals.

Use it when the situation is real but still manageable. Work slowly, keep one written decision log, and prefer the safest reversible action over a dramatic improvisation.

Core sequence: 1) Pause at the safest outside edge and listen for hissing, sparking, running water, cracking, or animal movement. 2) Smell for gas, smoke, sewage, chemicals, mold, or spoiled food before touching switches or doors. 3) Look for sagging ceilings, shifted walls, standing water, broken glass, or insects before stepping in. 4) Leave immediately and call the right authority if smell, sound, structure, water, or electricity suggests hidden danger.

Stop point: Do not use return-home smell and sound inspection to enter a restricted, contaminated, structurally unsafe, or officially closed area. Main warnings: Do not continue return-home smell and sound inspection after the stop signs named in the steps appear. Do not let urgency turn return-home smell and sound inspection into a task done alone without a visible check-in.
materials: camera or notebook for visible observations
closed shoes and gloves ready but not yet used inside
contact numbers for utilities and building owner
steps: Pause at the safest outside edge and listen for hissing, sparking, running water, cracking, or animal movement.
Smell for gas, smoke, sewage, chemicals, mold, or spoiled food before touching switches or doors.
Look for sagging ceilings, shifted walls, standing water, broken glass, or insects before stepping in.
Leave immediately and call the right authority if smell, sound, structure, water, or electricity suggests hidden danger.
warnings: Do not continue return-home smell and sound inspection after the stop signs named in the steps appear.
Do not let urgency turn return-home smell and sound inspection into a task done alone without a visible check-in.
common_mistakes: Leaving return-home smell and sound inspection undocumented makes the next person repeat the same risk.
Ignoring the first small warning during return-home smell and sound inspection often creates a larger recovery problem.
alternatives: If the normal method for return-home smell and sound inspection is blocked, do the smallest safe observation and return to a known safe point.
Ask a second person to read back the decision when stress or darkness affects return-home smell and sound inspection.
failure_signs: There is no named owner for return-home smell and sound inspection after the first action.
The environment changes faster than the group can record the result of return-home smell and sound inspection.
when_not_to_use: Do not use return-home smell and sound inspection to enter a restricted, contaminated, structurally unsafe, or officially closed area.
Do not continue return-home smell and sound inspection if it delays urgent medical care or an emergency call.
short_term: Complete one documented cycle of return-home smell and sound inspection and confirm that the next person can repeat it safely.
long_term: Review return-home smell and sound inspection after each real disruption and update the route, storage, contact, or stop rule that failed.
if_method_fails: If return-home smell and sound inspection fails, retreat to the last safe state, write the reason, and choose a smaller action with a named owner.
environment_notes: Adjust return-home smell and sound inspection for heat, rain, smoke, darkness, crowds, language barriers, limited mobility, and unreliable power.
